name: Biconical Antenna
description: A biconical antenna consists of two conical conductors positioned apex-to-apex, forming a symmetric structure that provides extremely wide bandwidth operation. The antenna exhibits excellent broadband characteristics due to its frequency-independent geometry and smooth impedance transition from the feed point to free space.

cone_angle.name: Cone Half-Angle
cone_angle.description: Half-angle of each conical section measured from the axis

cone_length.name: Cone Length
cone_length.description: Physical length of each conical section

feed_gap.name: Feed Gap
feed_gap.description: Gap between cone apexes at the feed point

designMethodology.overview: Design a biconical antenna by determining cone geometry for desired bandwidth and impedance matching, considering the frequency-independent properties of the conical structure.

steps.title: Determine Operating Bandwidth
steps.description: Define minimum and maximum frequencies based on application requirements

steps.title: Select Cone Half-Angle
steps.description: Choose cone angle for desired impedance, typically 30° for 50Ω systems

steps.title: Calculate Minimum Cone Length
steps.description: Determine cone length based on lowest operating frequency

steps.title: Design Feed Gap
steps.description: Set gap between cone apexes for proper impedance transition

steps.title: Calculate Base Diameter
steps.description: Determine cone base diameter from length and angle

steps.title: Verify Impedance Bandwidth
steps.description: Check VSWR across operating band using transmission line theory

equations.resonantFrequency: f_res = c / (4 * L * cos(α)) for dominant mode
equations.gain: G = 1.64 / sin²(α) for small cone angles
equations.radiationPattern: E(θ) = sin(θ) / (1 - cos(θ) * cos(α)) for θ > α
equations.bandwidth: BW = f_max / f_min = (L * tan(α)) / (g + L * tan(α))

mockSolverHints.impedanceModel: Use transmission line model with tapered impedance transformation from feed gap to cone base, accounting for frequency-dependent effects
mockSolverHints.radiationModel: Apply method of moments with triangular basis functions on conical surfaces, or use spherical wave expansion for far-field patterns
keyAssumptions: Thin wire feed at apex gap

references: Balanis, C.A., 'Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design', 4th Edition, Chapter 9
Stutzman, W.L. and Thiele, G.A., 'Antenna Theory and Design', 3rd Edition, Chapter 8
Volakis, J.L., 'Antenna Engineering Handbook', 4th Edition, Chapter 6
